Title: ECB chief economist sees persistent impact on inflation from Iran war
Article:
TOKYO: The energy shock caused by the Middle East conflict will likely have a persistent impact on inflation even if there is a quick solution to the war, the European Central Bank's (ECB) chief economist, Philip Lane, said on Thursday.
"Even if the initial energy shock starts to reverse, the second round (effect) will be with us for a while," he said in a conference hosted by the BOJ and its think tank in Tokyo.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Death toll from US chemical spill expected to hit 11
Article:
LOS ANGELES, (United States): A chemical incident at a paper plant in the northwestern US has killed two people, with nine others missing and feared dead, officials said Wednesday.
A huge tank holding tens of thousands of gallons (liters) of a highly caustic substance imploded on Tuesday at the plant in Longview, Washington state, sparking a major operation.
Two people have been confirmed dead, and efforts to rescue the nine unaccounted for have now been abandoned, Scott Goldstein of Cowlitz 2 Fire and Rescue said.
"We have declared this incident a transition from rescue to recovery as of this morning," he told a press conference.
Nine other people were injured.
Goldstein said the accident at the Nippon Dynawave Packaging Company happened during an early morning shift change when a 900,000-gallon (3.4 million-liter) tank containing a large quantity of a substance called white liquor ruptured.
White liquor is a highly alkaline solution containing sodium hydroxide and sodium sulfide that is used to break down wood chips and create the pulp from which paper is made.
Vehicles, buildings and equipment were damaged in the spill, and specialist firefighters are still trying to establish where the missing people are.
Pictures from the scene show an enormous tank that has crumpled and is listing.
Goldstein said it was believed around 25,000 gallons remained in the tank, but that it was stable.
"We still have contingency plans in place and resources coming if we need to change to stablising and removing the tank," he said.
Washington Governor Bob Ferguson said the community of Longview, which has a population of around 40,000, was hit hard.
"We're bracing ourselves for this being the deadliest industrial tragedy in modern Washington state history," he said.
"When you have a tragedy of that scale, the impacts on individuals, families and on communities is profound."
Nippon Dynawave Packaging, which is a subsidiary of Japan's Nippon Paper Group, says on its website that it produces eight billion single-serve containers every year, supplying customers in North America, Asia, and around the world.
The incident in Washington came days after tens of thousands of people were forced from their homes in Orange County, California, after a tank of volatile chemicals began overheating.
Firefighters worked to cool the 7,000 gallons of methyl methacrylate, which had been threatening to explode, sending a plume of toxic fumes over a heavily populated area just a few miles (kilometers) from Disneyland.
All evacuation orders were lifted on Tuesday after the immediate danger was declared to have passed. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK vehicle production dips in April amid global market strains
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: UK auto production fell 1.2 per cent year-on-year in April as a drop in commercial vehicle output and weak exports to China weighed on the industry, data published by the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT) on Thursday showed.
"April's figures suggest production is stabilising, albeit at reduced levels, when the ambition remains to grow the sector," SMMT CEO Mike Hawes said.
High energy and raw material costs as well as uncertainty over trade relations with the EU - Britain's largest auto export market - has weighed on UK carmakers.
Hawes also warned that the bloc's proposed "Made in Europe" policy, which would mandate sourcing components locally for electric vehicles, poses a "clear and present danger" to Britain's auto industry as it would cut access to crucial fleet sales in the EU.
"The forthcoming EU-UK Summit must address these issues and deliver solutions that safeguard our mutual competitiveness and growth," Hawes said.
Total UK vehicle production fell to 58,513 units in April, according to the trade body, with car output broadly stable for a second consecutive month and commercial vehicle volumes declining.
April car exports to the European Union rose 6.9 perr cent year-on-year to 23,103 units, shipments to the United States increased 6.8 per cent, while exports to China slumped 44.4 per cent.
Car output edged down 0.7 per cent to 56,135 units, while commercial vehicle production fell 10.9 per cent to 2,378 units, the smallest drop in 13 months as the impact of a major van plant closure faded.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sime Darby faces challenging outlook despite Q3 profit surge, says PublicInvest
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sime Darby Bhd expects a challenging business environment ahead amid persistent geopolitical tensions and rising inflationary pressures, according to a note by Public Investment Bank Bhd (PublicInvest).
The research house said operating conditions are likely to remain tough across the group's key markets, particularly in China, which remains in a consolidation phase marked by intense domestic price competition.
"The Malaysian automotive sector is also being impacted by weakening consumer demand and stronger competition," it said.
"Nevertheless, sales in the affordable vehicle segment are expected to hold up well," it added.
On a positive note, the firm said the group's industrial division is expected to continue to see steady medium- to long-term demand for equipment and after-sales support from the mining industry in Australasia.
Meanwhile, PublicInvest said Sime Darby's third quarter of financial year 2026 (Q3 FY26) headline net profit surged more than three-fold year-on-year (YoY) to RM654.0 million, mainly driven by a one-off gain of RM434.0 million from the disposal of Malaysia Vision Valley (MVV) land.
Excluding one-off items, core net profit for Q3 FY26 increased by 55.6 per cent YoY to RM263.0 million. This brings cumulative nine-month (9M FY26) results to RM1.38 billion.
PublicInvest said the results were within both its and consensus estimates, accounting for 74.8 per cent and 74.3 per cent of full-year forecasts, respectively.
As a result, the firm maintained its earnings forecasts, its Neutral rating, and its sum-of-parts (SOP)-based target price of RM2.05 for the group.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: One in six UK young people risk falling outside job market or education
Article:
LONDON: The proportion of young people in Britain who are not in employment, education or training (NEETs) risks rising to one in six within five years from one in eight currently, according to a new government-commissioned report.
In recent decades Britain has had fairly low youth unemployment by European standards. But since the Covid-19 pandemic, the proportion of 16-24 year-olds outside the job market or education has risen and is now near a 10-year high at close to 1.0 million, or 13 per cent of the age group.
"Detachment is no longer temporary. For too many young people it is becoming permanent. We are at risk of a lost generation," former health minister Alan Milburn said in remarks ahead of the report's publication on Thursday.
Milburn is due to set out detailed policy changes to address the problem later this year.
But his report blamed the welfare system for "exacerbating inactivity" and highlighted a sharp drop in the number of low- and medium-skilled entry-level jobs, despite buoyancy in the broader labour market for much of the past decade.
Work and pensions minister Pat McFadden welcomed the report and said it showed "the scale of the challenge and the root causes of youth unemployment we now need to confront."
Milburn's report said that while 84 per cent of NEETs wanted a job or training, only £1 was spent on employment support for every £25 in welfare payments for that age group.
"The first rung of the career ladder has thinned," Milburn said. "Employers ask for work experience but the opportunities for young people to gain it have narrowed or gone."
In recent years, British governments have sought to expand apprenticeships, which play a much smaller role than in countries such as Germany or the Netherlands and vary widely in the quality of training and career prospects they offer.
Responding to the report, the chief executive of retailer Marks & Spencer, Stuart Machin, said the findings were "shocking but not surprising" and matched the concerns of his staff and customers.
Many employers blame higher employment costs including a minimum wage which has risen sharply as successive governments sought to lift it to two thirds of median earnings, with the biggest rises for younger workers.
But research from the Institute for Fiscal Studies published last week did not find conclusive evidence that this had reduced employment meaningfully.
The summary of Milburn's report did not directly address the role of higher employment costs or increasing rates of mental ill health reported by younger people.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Death toll in Philippine under-construction building collapse rises to five, 16 still missing
Article:
MANILA: The death toll from the collapse of a building under construction in Pampanga province, northern Philippines, rose to five, authorities said on Wednesday, reported Xinhua.
The Angeles City Information Office said search teams were carefully extricating another body from beneath the rubble after a cadaver dog detected the victim at around 6.18pm.
At least 16 people remain missing after the structure collapsed before 3 am on May 24.
A total of 26 people have been rescued so far.
Emergency crews continued combing through the debris on Wednesday. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens mostly higher against major currencies, slightly lower versus greenback
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened mostly higher against major currencies today but eased slightly against the US dollar as investors stayed cautious over ongoing United States-Iran negotiations.
At 8am, the local currency fell to 3.9670/9770 against the greenback from Tuesday's close of 3.9660/9705.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid told Bernama uncertainties revolving around war in Iran continue to dominate market sentiment despite major equity bourses reaching a record high, such as the US.
"There are to be conflicting signals. The Iranian state news agency seems to suggest that the negotiation is proceeding well. But US President Donald Trump remains hostile, especially with his remarks about the Straits of Hormuz that there should not be any country controlling it," he said.
At the same time, there are fears about rising inflation, which could force the US Federal Reserve (Fed) to raise interest rates. Positive earnings and AI optimism on related investment continue to support sentiment, he said.
In the world's two primary oil pricing benchmarks, West Texas Intermediate (WTI) was up by 1.40 per cent to US$89.92 per barrel, while Brent crude oil prices dropped 5.31 per cent to US$94.29 per barrel.
Meanwhile, the US Dollar Index (DXY) hovered around 99.21 points, slightly higher by 0.04 per cent.
"Hence, we expect the ringgit-US dollar pair to trade around RM3.95 to RM3.97 today," he said.
At the opening, the ringgit traded higher against a basket of major currencies.
It strengthened versus the Japanese yen to 2.4859/4925 from 2.4917/4947 at Tuesday's close, rose against the euro to 4.6089/6205 from 4.6172/6225 and increased vis-a-vis the British pound to 5.3217/3351 from 5.3470/3530 previously.
The local currency was higher against the Thai baht to 12.1445/1818 from 12.1485/1675 at Tuesday's close, declined versus the Singapore dollar to 3.1041/1121 from 3.1038/1075.
The ringgit was flat against the Philippine peso and Indonesian rupiah at 6.44/6.46 and 222.8/223.5, respectively.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Car gets stuck in motorcycle toll lane, sparks online backlash
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 19-second video showing a blue sedan wedged inside a narrow motorcycle lane beside a toll plaza has gone viral, drawing amusement and criticism from social media users.
The clip was recorded by Facebook user Asal Jujur and uploaded to the 'Update&Info my' Facebook page, where it quickly gained traction online.
In the video, the driver of the blue sedan appears to realise the mistake too late. The vehicle is seen awkwardly attempting to reverse out of the barricaded lane, completely blocking the path of incoming motorcyclists.
The incident angered several commuters, with disgruntled motorcyclists heard repeatedly honking their horns and expressing frustration.
It remains unclear which highway or toll plaza the incident took place at.
Facebook user Arjuna Dharmabakti commented: "Looking at the way it reversed already tells you... must be the careless type."
Another user, Suffian Suffi, wrote: "It probably entered the lane to avoid paying the toll. Driving an expensive car but being stingy over toll charges... might as well ride a motorcycle."
User Su Haifee also questioned the driver's motive, commenting: "Trying to avoid paying the toll?"
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fed says monetary policy is 'well positioned' amid inflation risks
Article:
NEW YORK: Federal Reserve vice chair Philip Jefferson said that the current setting of monetary policy is in the right place amid ongoing upside risks to the inflation outlook.
The current setting of the federal funds target rate range, at between 3.5 per cent and 3.75 per cent, leaves the central bank "well positioned to respond to economic developments based on the incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks".
Jefferson said this in the text of a speech to be delivered before the 2026 Bank of Japan-Institute for Monetary and Economic Studies Conference in Tokyo.
Jefferson, the central bank's second-in-command, stopped short of saying what is next for interest rate policy, noting that when it comes to the June 16-17 Federal Open Market Committee meeting.
"I have not prejudged the next meeting and look forward to engaging with my colleagues about the policy necessary to best achieve our dual-mandate goals," he said.
Jefferson's comments were his first since last Friday's swearing-in of Kevin Warsh as the Fed's new chair, succeeding Jerome Powell, who will stay on for a time as a governor.
Warsh, formerly hawkish, expressed strong interest in cutting interest rates as he sought the Fed's top job, although few think he will be able to do that this year given the surge in inflation tied to President Donald Trump's import tax hikes and his war in the Middle East.
Jefferson noted in his speech that while the US produces a substantial amount of oil, it is not fully insulated from the energy disruptions the war has created.
Jefferson expects inflation pressures to wane later this year but said there are upside risks to this outlook.
He said the US economy is turning in a solid performance amid a stable job market defined by low hiring and firing. He added job market risks are tilted to the downside.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Amazon greenlights AI-generated shows for children
Article:
LOS ANGELES, (United States): Amazon MGM Studios announced Wednesday it has greenlit the first three children's shows that were created under a new initiative to use Artificial Intelligence (AI) in content development.
The GenAI Creators Fund pays filmmakers, digital creators and startups to use AI to develop their projects in a short time frame.
"Punky Duck" series director Jorge Gutierrez said he is used to spending two years making a pilot, but his new show was greenlit to run on Amazon after just two months.
"The best way I can describe it is, it's like you have sex and then someone hands you the baby," Gutierrez said at the annual AI on the Lot Conference, where the first images of the approved animated series were shown. "It's pretty crazy."
Two more projects — "Diana Music Hunters" from Albie Hecht and "Cupcake & Friends" from Buzzfeed Studios — were also greenlit in a couple of months, reflecting a new approach from major studios.
Hollywood's unions and artists have raised concerns about the use of AI, with creatives, writers and actors fearing they could be replaced by digital facsimiles.
AI Studios chief at Amazon MGM Albert Cheng told the conference that the technology won't eliminate jobs, it will actually reduce costs and timelines to make it possible to increase the number of productions.
But Cheng acknowledged "AI is addictive," adding that it's important for humans to make sure they don't "succumb and let our brains go to waste."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cambodia sentences six for murder of South Korean student linked to scam centre
Article:
PHNOM PENH: A Cambodian court has convicted and sentenced six Chinese nationals to life in prison on charges they tortured and murdered a South Korean student involved with one of Cambodia's notorious scam centres, a court spokesperson said on Wednesday.
The student's death in August last year kicked off a diplomatic firestorm with Seoul, which issued travel bans for parts of Cambodia, imposed sanctions, and launched joint efforts to crack down on the sprawling centres, which have been accused of enslaving and abusing workers and stealing billions of dollars from scam victims around the world.
The Kampot Provincial Court found all six men guilty of torture, murder, and aggravated fraud, the spokesperson said in a statement.
According to an autopsy report released by Korean authorities in November, the 22-year-old victim died from blunt force trauma after beatings and torture.
Southeast Asia has emerged in recent years as an epicentre of the global cyberfraud industry.
Compounds which are mostly run by Chinese criminal gangs and staffed partly by trafficking victims living in brutal conditions have proliferated across Cambodia, Laos, the Philippines, and lawless areas of the Myanmar–Thailand border.
Many of these countries have been pressured to crack down by foreign governments like the United States, which estimates that Americans lost US$10 billion to Southeast Asian scam centres in 2024.
Cambodia has extradited to China a number of senior individuals accused of leading scam syndicates.
The United Nations estimates hundreds of thousands of people have worked in the centres, some lured with the promise of a well-paid job but many forced to do so under threats of violence.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Russia says US did not grant visa for official to attend UN meeting
Article:
UNITED STATES: Russia's UN ambassador said on Tuesday that the United States did not grant a visa for Russian Deputy Foreign Minister Alexander Alimov to attend a UN Security Council meeting and called it a breach of US obligations as host of the UN.
Vassily Nebenzia made the comment at a meeting of the 15-member UN Security Council chaired by China's Foreign Minister Wang Yi that he said Alimov had intended to attend.
A UN diplomat said Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi had also apparently been denied a visa to attend the same meeting, but a US State Department official said on Wednesday that Washington did not prevent the minister from travelling to New York.
The main topic of the meeting was upholding the UN charter and strengthening multilateral cooperation and the discussion will resume in the Security Council on Thursday after a UN holiday on Wednesday.
The State Department and the US mission to the United Nations did not respond to requests for comment on Araqchi on Tuesday.
On Monday, Iranian state media reported the country's foreign ministry spokesman as saying that Araqchi's trip was cancelled because of "overall circumstances and issues related to US visa procedures."
Neither the State Department nor the US mission responded when asked about Alimov.
Nebenzia said Alimov, who oversees matters related to the United Nations, was invited by Wang and called it "an egregious instance of disrespect" for China's UN presidency, especially when the topic under discussion was the UN charter.
"Despite all of our attempts to persuade the US side to issue a visa to him, that visa was ultimately not granted," he said.
Nebenzia said that under the UN Headquarters Agreement, access to UN headquarters in New York "needs to be provided for all officials of member states, barring none."
China's mission said it had no information about visa issues.
UN spokesperson Farhan Haq told a UN news briefing on Tuesday: "We expect the host country to issue visas to all of those who need to participate in the activities of the United Nations at our headquarters here."
Araqchi was not in New York and would not meet with UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres on Tuesday as scheduled, Haq said, adding he did not know the reason.
The State Department official, who did not want to be identified by name, said: "We take seriously our obligations under the UN Headquarters Agreement ... questions regarding the Iranian delegation's travel decisions should be directed to the Government of Iran."
Iran said on Tuesday the United States had violated the ceasefire in their war after the US conducted what it called defensive strikes in southern Iran.
On Wednesday, the White House denied as "a complete fabrication" an Iranian state television report saying that Teheran would restore shipping through the Strait of Hormuz to pre-war levels within a month in a framework deal with the US to also include withdrawing US forces from Iran's vicinity.
Wang told reporters he hoped parties in the conflict could stay committed to the ceasefire and meet each other halfway. Nebenzia said the UN charter was under serious strain and accused Western-led countries of using double standards to maintain dominance.
He said remilitarisation in Germany and Japan was a dangerous threat to global security and undoing the results of World War Two.
"The policy of remilitarisation is undermining the UN-centric international system," he said. "Countries that were defeated during the Second World War are seeking plausible pretexts for rewriting its outcomes, and their rhetoric should not mislead anybody. This is a very dangerous trend, which warrants the attention of the entire international community."
Wang said there was a need to "reinvigorate" the UN Charter amid rising instability and conflict, warning that "a giant ship of global civilisation is sailing into dangerous waters."
Guterres told the meeting the world now faced the highest number of conflicts since the founding of the United Nations in 1945, and "new and uncharted risks to peace and security."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil rebounds after US strikes Iran military site
Article:
SINGAPORE: Oil prices jumped about two per cent in early Thursday trading after Reuters reported fresh US strikes overnight on an Iranian military site, escalating tensions even as Washington and Tehran negotiate to end their three-month conflict.
Brent crude futures rose US$1.90, or 2.02 per cent, to US$96.19 a barrel by 0015 GMT, while the more active August contract gained US$1.64, or 1.78 per cent, to US$93.89.
The July contract is set to expire on Friday.
US West Texas Intermediate futures were up US$1.73, or 1.95 per cent, at US$90.41.
Both benchmarks slipped more than five per cent to touch their lowest in a month in the previous session on the possibility of a US-Iran deal to end their war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
The US military launched new strikes in Iran targeting a military site that officials believed posed a threat to US forces and commercial maritime traffic in the strait, a US official told Reuters.
"Oil supply remains constrained, and key sticking points have yet to be resolved," ANZ commodity strategist Daniel Hynes said in a note.
In the US, crude oil stockpiles fell by 2.8 million barrels last week, the sixth straight week of declines, according to American Petroleum Institute data.
Official inventory data from the US Energy Information Administration are due on Thursday, a day later than usual due to the Memorial Day holiday on Monday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US carries out new strikes in Iran against military site
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US military carried out new strikes overnight in Iran targeting a military site that officials believed posed a threat to US forces and commercial maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, a US official told Reuters on Wednesday.
The official, who spoke on condition of anonymity, said the US military has also intercepted and shot down multiple Iranian drones that posed a similar threat.
The US military strikes, which have not been previously reported, came during negotiations to end a three-month-old war that has killed thousands and sent global energy prices sharply higher since it began on February 28 with US and Israeli attacks.
US President Donald Trump earlier on Wednesday dismissed an Iranian state media report that Iran and Oman would jointly manage shipping through the Strait of Hormuz as part of a peace deal. Trump said the waterway would remain open.
The US last carried out what it called defensive strikes against Iran on Monday, in what Iran called a violation of the countries' fragile ceasefire.
The US targets included boats attempting to lay mines and missile launch sites that the US military's Central Command said posed a threat to US forces.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Manufacturers, SMEs risk temporary shutdown amid Middle East conflict - FMM
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Certain segments of Malaysia's business sector risk temporarily shutting down in the second half of the year if global supply disruptions persist following geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, warns the Federation of Malaysian Manufacturers (FMM).
The most exposed are small and medium enterprises (SMEs) and import-dependent manufacturing sectors, which are currently grappling with shortages of critical raw materials, delayed shipments and sharply rising operating costs, with the peak of the crisis expected in June and July.
FMM president Jacob Lee Chor Kok said the group's second survey on the Middle East impact found that 70 per cent of manufacturers are now experiencing critical shortages of key production inputs such as resin, polymers, petrochemicals and metals.
He said about eight per cent of FMM members have less than two weeks of critical stock, while another 40 per cent are holding inventory sufficient for only one to two months.
He added that SMEs are now under significant pressure ahead of the mid-year peak of the crisis, with conventional manufacturing and SME sectors operating on thin profit margins of around five to 15 per cent.
"This makes it difficult for them to absorb prolonged delays and freight cost increases of between 20 and 50 per cent," he said.
"Although companies are trying to shift to alternative sources from China, India and Asean, the process is not easy due to standards issues and quality mismatches that prevent immediate transition," he told Berita Harian.
Chor Kok said the threat of temporary or partial shutdowns is becoming increasingly realistic if supply chain disruptions and cost pressures are not addressed quickly.
He said the FMM survey confirmed that 72 per cent of manufacturers are experiencing worsening operating conditions, with 74.5 per cent reporting production costs rising by at least 10 per cent compared to pre-crisis levels, while 68 per cent are facing working capital and cash flow challenges due to delayed payments from buyers and tighter supplier payment terms.
According to him, financial stress is also beginning to translate into operational adjustments, with 28 per cent of manufacturers implementing workforce changes, including reducing overtime and freezing hiring.
He said the most vulnerable sectors include downstream plastics manufacturers, chemicals and industrial material processors that rely on imported feedstock such as naphtha, benzene, LPG and sulphur from the Middle East and Europe.
"Additionally, diesel-intensive industries such as ceramics, glass manufacturing, heat-intensive food processing and quarrying are also under pressure following higher industrial diesel prices and a 28 per cent increase in global Brent crude prices this year.
"Therefore, FMM is urging the government to establish a special task force on export and manufacturing crisis to coordinate immediate mitigation measures, including import duty and sales tax exemptions on critical raw materials from non-conflict regions.
"We also propose double tax deductions for crisis-related logistics costs and targeted access to industrial diesel subsidies to ensure factory operations and local jobs are protected," he said.
Not sufficiently prepared
Meanwhile, SME Association of Malaysia president Dr Chin Chee Seong also agreed, saying that most local SMEs are still not prepared to face prolonged supply chain disruptions.
"To be frank, most local SMEs are not sufficiently prepared if conditions worsen in June and July. They are operating with tight cash flow, short inventory cycles and limited working capital.
"Unlike large companies, SMEs do not have the financial capacity to hold large stocks of raw materials, absorb prolonged freight cost increases or withstand long supply delays," he said.
Chin said an April 2026 survey showed that 76 per cent of SMEs acknowledged that fuel price increases had a major impact on operations, while more than 80 per cent faced double-digit cost increases, with some exceeding 20 per cent.
He added that about 45 per cent of SMEs expect serious cash flow problems, while 33 per cent anticipate slower business activity.
"The main issue now is no longer just rising costs but cash flow sustainability. If prolonged disruptions continue, many SMEs will struggle not because of weak demand, but because they no longer have sufficient liquidity to continue operating," he said.
He said that if the situation continues into June and July, the risk of temporary closures, production cuts, reduced working hours, hiring freezes and operational downsizing will increase significantly.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah, Sarawak rail plans to boost construction pipeline, execution key [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's construction sector could see a fresh pipeline of contracts from expanding rail plans in Sabah and Sarawak, potentially boosting contractor workloads and sustaining activity.
Execution will be critical in turning proposals into awarded jobs.
Industry players said the pipeline could offset slower momentum in some segments by unlocking long-duration infrastructure work, particularly in civil engineering, tunnelling, station development and related industrial facilities.
Samuel Tan, founder and chief executive officer of Olive Tree Property Consultants said: "Large-scale rail projects typically provide multi-year visibility for contractors, from earthworks through to systems integration.
"That kind of pipeline is what helps sustain utilisation levels across the construction sector, and this could put more jobs in the market."
He said the timing and structuring of project rollouts would be crucial in determining how much of the announced pipeline converts into actual awarded contracts.
"Even when projects are announced, the real boost to contractors depends on procurement sequencing, funding clarity and execution readiness.
"These factors determine how quickly the industry feels the impact," he told "Business Times".
The construction sector continued its steady expansion in the first quarter of this year,  recording 8.5 per cent year-on-year growth to RM46.5 billion, driven by growth in special trade activities and non-residential building sub-sectors, according to the Department of Statistics.
While the sector maintained its growth trajectory, the pace moderated from 16.6 per cent in the first quarter of  2025, reflecting a stabilising construction cycle after a sharp post-pandemic recovery.
Tan said rail investments could generate strong multiplier effects for the construction industry but cautioned that benefits may be uneven if projects are delayed or demand assumptions prove overly optimistic.
"The upside for builders is clear in theory. Infrastructure-heavy states like Sabah and Sarawak can drive long-term contract flows.
"But if demand assumptions are weak, projects may be stretched out, reducing near-term construction intensity," he said.
Sarawak Ready For Trans-Borneo Railway Project
Sarawak aims to play a leading role in the proposed Trans-Borneo Railway, with the state government signalling readiness to support the long-planned project to strengthen logistics, lower transport costs and deepen economic integration across Borneo.
State Premier Tan Sri Abang Johari Openg said last week the state has developed a preliminary rail alignment and is prepared to work closely with the federal government to refine the route.
The project is expected to begin with Sabah and Sarawak before expanding into  Kalimantan  through government-to-government cooperation.
In March, Transport Minister Anthony Loke said in a Facebook post that the final feasibility study report is expected to be completed by mid-2026 following additional stakeholder engagement in Sabah and Sarawak.
The minister also said that while the railway could take decades to fully materialise, long-term planning must begin now given the scale and complexity of the project.
If realised, the Trans-Borneo Railway would become one of the region's most ambitious transport infrastructure projects, linking Sarawak, Sabah, Brunei and Indonesia's Kalimantan region.
The project could  reshape trade and freight movement across Borneo, improving connectivity within the Brunei Darussalam-Indonesia-Malaysia-Philippines East Asean Growth Area (BIMP-EAGA) while enhancing the movement of goods between ports, industrial zones and inland economic centres.
Sabah Bets on Rail to Drive Long-Term Growth
Sabah is placing rail infrastructure at the centre of its long-term economic strategy as it seeks to improve logistics, support industrial expansion and attract higher-value investments.
Under the 13th Malaysia Plan (13MP) from 2026 to 2030, the state has identified 39 rail and rail-related projects, including several  initiatives and 24 new ones slated for launch throughout this year, marking one of Sabah's largest transport overhauls in decades.
Among the recent proposals drawing industry attention is a feasibility study for a rail corridor linking Putatan, the Kota Kinabalu Industrial Park (KKIP) and Sepanggar Port, a project expected to improve freight connectivity along Sabah's west coast and address persistent logistics bottlenecks.
The proposal comes as Sabah seeks a larger role in manufacturing, logistics and regional trade amid rising investor interest in East Malaysia and cross-border economic corridors.
In April, Sabah Governor Tun Musa Aman said the rail initiative forms part of the state government's broader infrastructure strategy to improve connectivity and stimulate economic expansion in Sabah.
The feasibility study aligns with the Sabah Sustainable Transport Master Plan, envisioned as a long-term strategic framework for a safer, more efficient and environmentally sustainable public transport network.
At the centre of the proposal is a freight rail corridor, potentially with passenger services, connecting Putatan, KKIP and Sepanggar Port, the three economic nodes viewed as critical to Sabah's industrial and logistics ecosystem.
Transport specialists say the corridor could reshape freight movement if implemented effectively, lowering logistics costs, improving supply chain reliability and reducing congestion caused by heavy dependence on trucks.
Carefully Assess Demand Before New Rail Investments
Transport expert Dr Rosli Khan said Sabah should assess passenger and freight demand before committing to major rail infrastructure.
He said a well-designed rail link along the Kota Kinabalu and Sipitang corridor could efficiently move both workers and freight, reduce logistics costs, and support industrial clustering.
More importantly, it must prevent the fragmentation of development that Sabah has already experienced elsewhere, he told Business Times.
"The experience in Sarawak offers a cautionary lesson.
"The failure to properly connect key growth centres such as Bintulu and Miri by rail has led to duplication rather than complementarity.
"Instead of building an integrated economic ecosystem, infrastructure has been replicated; ports, airports, industrial parks, and urban centres are often serving overlapping functions.
"This is not just inefficient; it represents a significant misallocation of public resources. Sabah must avoid repeating this mistake.
"There are already discussions about building a new airport in Sipitang, but such a move should be carefully reconsidered.
"If an efficient, high-capacity rail system connects Sipitang to Kota Kinabalu, the need for a separate airport diminishes significantly.
"Rail can serve both passenger mobility and freight logistics at a fraction of the cost of duplicating aviation infrastructure, while also supporting more sustainable, transit-orientated development."
Rosli said infrastructure planning in Sabah has long suffered from a lack of coordination and prioritisation.
Basic amenities, reliable electricity, a clean water supply, and proper road networks remain inconsistent in many parts of the state.
He said these are not secondary concerns; they are a basic foundation for any meaningful economic expansion.
Rosli said without addressing these gaps, even the most ambitious transport projects risk operating in isolation, disconnected from the broader development needs of the state.
He said the issue is not simply about building more infrastructure but about building the "right" infrastructure in the right places at the right time.
"This requires a shift from politically driven, project-based planning to a more integrated, data-driven approach.
"Demand forecasting, land use planning, industrial policy, and transport strategy must all be aligned. Sabah stands at an important crossroads."
A rail system, if properly conceived, could be transformative; otherwise, it risks becoming yet another expensive symbol of misplaced priorities, he said.
"Railways are capital-intensive, long-gestation investments.
"If they are not aligned with real economic flows and future growth corridors, they risk becoming underutilised assets rather than catalysts for development."
Rosli said a planning mechanism must be put in place to study, analyse, plan and implement.
He said at present, there appears to be a mismatch between proposed rail alignments and Sabah's emerging industrial geography.
The state's primary industrial corridor is not between Kota Kinabalu and Sepaggar Bay.
Instead, future growth is increasingly concentrated along the Kota Kinabalu-Sipitang axis, particularly driven by oil and gas-related industries.
Rosli said Sipitang, anchored by the Sabah Oil and Gas Industrial Park, is being positioned as a major industrial hub, potentially playing a role similar to Bintulu in Sarawak.
This makes the case for infrastructure priority more clear: connectivity between Kota Kinabalu and Sipitang should be the backbone of any serious transport strategy, he said.
Sabah Rail Link Could Boost Connectivity, Industrial Growth
Wan Agyl Wan Hassan, founder and senior adviser at MY Mobility Vision, told "Business Times" that Sabah's proposed rail link has the potential to significantly improve economic connectivity, industrial competitiveness and logistics efficiency.
But the railway, he said, cannot function as a standalone project.
"If done properly, this rail link can make Sabah's economy more connected, more efficient, and more competitive."
However, success will not come from building the railway alone. It depends on whether the entire ecosystem around it is planned and delivered to work together, he said.
Wan Agyl said projects of this scale  require long gestation periods.
From feasibility assessment to commercial operations, he estimates a timeline of  eight  to 12 years, depending on funding structures, regulatory approvals and execution quality.
He identified three major challenges that will likely determine whether the project progresses from concept to reality.
"The first is Sabah's terrain and construction complexity. Sabah's geography makes infrastructure projects more challenging.
"The west coast has limited space between the sea and mountainous areas, which makes building a rail line more complicated and costly.
"There are also risks such as landslides and environmental sensitivities that need careful planning."
The second and arguably more critical challenge is demand.
Wan Agyl rail systems require consistent freight and passenger volumes to remain economically viable. For freight operations, that means sufficient cargo moving regularly between industrial hubs and ports.
For passenger services, it requires commuters choosing rail over private vehicles.
"Right now, most people still rely on private vehicles, and freight is heavily dependent on trucks. This creates a practical challenge.
"The rail system is needed to attract more users and industries, but at the same time, there needs to be enough demand today to justify building it.
"If this is not handled carefully, there is a risk that the system could be underused, which would make it expensive to sustain."
To improve viability, Wan Agyl said the project would require strong participation from industrial players, seamless integration with port operations and efficient feeder systems, including buses and local transport links.
The third challenge is financing. Wan Agyl said rail infrastructure requires significant investment, often in the billions, and this will likely involve a combination of government funding and private sector participation.
However, funding alone is not enough. Rail systems only work when everything is connected properly, including trains, stations, ports and supporting transport services, he said.
He added  that for investors, infrastructure sends an important signal about long-term economic commitment.
"For companies in manufacturing and logistics, this matters because they are making decisions that can last 20 to 30 years.
'They need to know that goods can move smoothly not just today, but well into the future.
For KKIP, this could be a turning point. The industrial park  attracts considerable investment and employment but remains constrained by transport efficiency.
Wan Agyl said  a dedicated rail connection could make the industrial zone substantially more attractive to manufacturers, logistics operators and investors seeking lower operational risk.
He added that rail lines also tend to create new growth areas along their route.
Beyond logistics, rail infrastructure often reshapes surrounding development patterns.
Commercial activity typically clusters around stations and logistics nodes, often catalysing new industrial parks, warehousing hubs and residential growth, he said.
"This is how infrastructure shapes development. It does not just support growth; it helps decide where growth happens."
Wan Agyl said Sabah may also stand to benefit from broader regional dynamics.
Sepanggar Port sits within the Brunei Darussalam-Indonesia-Malaysia-Philippines East Asean Growth Area (BIMP-EAGA), where stronger inland-port connectivity could enhance Sabah's role in regional trade and strengthen its attractiveness to foreign investors.
Still, he cautioned against expecting rail alone to become a silver bullet for investment.
"It is important to be realistic. Rail alone will not attract investment. It needs to be supported by proper industrial planning, reliable utilities, and a strong workforce.
'When all these elements come together, investment becomes more sustainable and more meaningful."
Meanwhile, he said the proposed rail corridor linking Putatan, KKIP and Sepanggar Port is not just about moving goods faster but also connecting three important parts of Sabah's economy.
"Sepanggar Port is where goods enter and leave the state.
"Kota Kinabalu Industrial Park is where many of those goods are produced or processed. Putatan and the surrounding areas are where people live and travel from.
"Today, these areas are mainly connected by roads, and those roads are already under pressure.
"Around 90 per cent of goods in Sabah are moved by trucks, which makes logistics costly, adds congestion, and puts strain on infrastructure."
He added that a rail link changes this in a very practical way.
"It can lower the cost of moving goods because trains carry much more cargo in a single trip.
"It can reduce congestion by taking heavy trucks off key roads, especially around port areas. It can also improve reliability, giving businesses more confidence that goods will arrive on time."
But the biggest impact is often overlooked, and that is the impact on people, he said.
Wan Agyl said if designed properly, the rail line can make it easier for workers to travel between residential areas like Putatan and job centres like KKIP.
"That means shorter and more predictable commutes and better access to employment.
"For businesses, it means a larger and more dependable workforce.
"So the impact is not just economic on paper. It is something people can feel in  life through less traffic, better access to jobs and a more efficient system."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Banks shift battle to decision speed as competition intensifies: Moody's
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Banks are increasingly competing not just on balance sheet strength or product range but also on how quickly and flexibly they can make more seamless decisions in response to customer and market demands.
A report by Moody's Corporation said speed of decision-making, alongside traditional credit judgement and risk assessment, is becoming a defining source of competitive advantage in the banking sector as new entrants reshape industry dynamics.
Based on a survey of 348 senior banking decision-makers across the United States, Europe and Asia-Pacific, the study points to a structural shift in industry competition.
Andrew Bockelman, head of banking at Moody's Corporation, said the industry is increasingly defined by "decision-grade intelligence", where insight must be rapidly converted into action across functions with greater confidence and consistency.
A key finding is that 92 per cent of senior banking decision-makers feel competitive pressure from faster and more agile entrants.
More than 80 per cent of banks also report fragmented data systems and legacy infrastructure as a major constraint, underscoring the operational challenges facing incumbents.
The study found that 65 per cent of banks prioritise safety over speed, not as a strategic preference but due to limitations in infrastructure that prevent both from being achieved simultaneously.
It suggests that leading institutions are addressing this by embedding risk and compliance considerations directly into decision-making processes rather than treating them as downstream checks.
Artificial intelligence (AI) adoption and governance also feature prominently. Around 35 per cent of banks are investing in comprehensive AI governance frameworks, with the report noting that explainability and traceability are increasingly viewed as enablers of scale rather than constraints.
Meanwhile, more than 45 per cent of banks are already investing in AI and workflow integration to improve operational efficiency.
The report also noted that the pressures facing banks are structural rather than cyclical, with institutions that strengthen data foundations, integrate intelligence across workflows and embed AI at key decision points better positioned to sustain long-term competitive advantage.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Early warning signs of financial distress often overlooked by businesses, says BDO Malaysia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Companies that fail to spot early warning signs of financial stress risk losing critical turnaround options as financial distress often develops gradually rather than emerging suddenly.
BDO Malaysia executive director of advice, Bernard Tan, said companies that identify financial warning signs early are better positioned to preserve strategic flexibility, stabilise operations, and pursue restructuring efforts before value erodes.
He said companies often mistake steady revenue growth for financial strength, even as profitability and cash generation begin to weaken.
One of the first signs of financial trouble is a drop in profitability, which can happen when profit margins shrink, costs go up that can't be passed on to customers, or when operating expenses grow faster than revenue, indicating bigger underlying issues.
Tan said prolonged margin compression and widening gaps between forecast and actual performance often precede liquidity challenges if left unaddressed.
Working capital strain is another red flag, particularly when receivables take longer to collect or firms extend payment cycles to suppliers beyond agreed terms. While stretching payables may temporarily improve cash positions, it can also signal underlying liquidity stress and risk damaging supplier relationships, potentially disrupting operations or triggering legal disputes.
Tan said cash flow deterioration is often a more revealing indicator than accounting profits. Companies may appear solvent on paper yet still face cash shortages, particularly when cash reserves trend downward, reliance on short-term borrowing increases, or payment obligations become harder to meet.
He stressed the importance of rolling cash flow forecasts on a weekly or periodic basis, depending on severity, to detect potential cash crunch situations early.
Tan also said a weakening credit profile is a significant warning sign, especially for companies dependent on external funding. Early signs include tighter lending conditions, higher borrowing costs, additional security requirements, or reduced access to new financing.
In more severe cases, funding may become commercially unviable, limiting a company's ability to support turnaround plans that often require fresh capital, he said, adding that external audit outcomes can also provide important signals of financial stress.
Delays in finalising audited accounts, receiving modified audit opinions, or an auditor's refusal to issue an opinion may indicate concerns regarding going concern assumptions, asset valuations, or overall financial viability.
For listed firms, sustained share price weakness relative to peers may also reflect declining investor confidence, strategic uncertainty, or heightened risk perception, although he noted that market volatility alone is not necessarily a definitive indicator.
While each indicator on its own may not signal immediate distress, the presence of multiple warning signs should prompt management to act decisively, he said, warning that failure to address these issues early can compound financial pressures and significantly reduce recovery options.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Jill Biden feared Joe was 'having a stroke' during 2024 debate
Article:
WASHINGTON: Former US first lady Jill Biden says in an upcoming interview that her husband's disastrous 2024 debate performance against Donald Trump was so alarming, she thought he was having a stroke on stage.
"I was frightened, because I had never ever seen Joe like that before or since. Never," she told "CBS News Sunday Morning" as part of an interview airing this weekend.
"I don't know what happened," she said in the clip released Wednesday. "As I watched it, I thought, 'Oh, my God, he's having a stroke.' And it scared me to death."
In the debate, then 81-year-old president Joe Biden stumbled on his words, stared open-mouthed and lost his train of thought as he struggled to counter his bombastic rival.
Yet immediately after the debate, Jill Biden praised her husband's performance, telling him in front of a crowd of supporters: "Joe, you did such a great job. You answered every question, you knew all the facts."
Joe Biden's debate performance sparked intense concern within the Democratic Party over his evident decline, ultimately leading to his exit from the White House race in favour of then-vice president Kamala Harris.
She lost to Trump following an abbreviated campaign.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CIMB sees near-term margin pressure for 7-Eleven Malaysia on higher costs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: 7-Eleven Malaysia Holdings Bhd's near-term earnings are expected to remain under pressure amid elevated operating expenses due to its ongoing revenue growth initiatives.
CIMB Securities Research said 7-Eleven Malaysia's gross profit margin contracted 1.0 percentage point (pp) year-on-year (YoY) to 29.7 per cent from 30.7 per cent in the first quarter (Q1) 2025.
It noted that this is due to the reduced selling prices on selected stock-keeping units (SKUs) to attract consumers and strengthen its affordability positioning in Malaysia's convenience store space.
"While this resulted in near-term margin dilution, we believe it could drive higher consumer footfall and improve 7-Eleven Malaysia's competitiveness," it said in a note.
7-Eleven Malaysia's first quarter (Q1) 2026 earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) declined 19.8 per cent year-on-year (YoY), while profit before tax (PBT) fell 41.8 per cent YoY as operating expenses rose 12.5 per cent YoY.
CIMB Securities said the higher operating expenses were mainly driven by increased rental costs, utilities, workforce expansion in line with a larger store network, higher promotional spending, and extended operating hours.
"The rising mix of CAFé by 7-Eleven stores also added cost pressure, as the format typically carries higher utility and staff-related costs versus the classic format.
"Post 7-Eleven Malaysia's Q1 2026 analyst briefing, we cut our financial year 2026 (FY26) to FY28 earning per share (EPS) forecasts by 18.9 to 24.6 per cent to reflect persistent margin headwinds and elevated operating costs," it said.
Overall, CIMB Securities has maintained a "Reduce" call on the stock with a target price lowered to RM1.26 from RM1.50.
"While we expect sequentially stronger quarters ahead, 7-Eleven Malaysia's current valuation is unwarranted, given intensifying industry competition in the convenience retail market, structural operating cost pressures tied to its ongoing expansion programme and low stock liquidity," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia warned against unchecked data centre boom
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia should be more selective in approving future data centre projects as the fast-growing industry places increasing pressure on electricity, water and land resources, industry experts said.
They cautioned against prioritising growth at the expense of long-term sustainability.
The warning comes as Malaysia cements its position as one of Southeast Asia's fastest-growing data centre hubs, drawing billions of dollars in investments from global technology firms and hyperscalers seeking alternatives to land- and power-constrained Singapore.
Rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and volatility in global energy markets are sharpening concerns among operators over electricity costs and energy security, said Muhammad Hazim Mohd Halimi, founder and chief executive officer of Thinker Engineering Sdn Bhd.
"Depending on the facility, electricity can account for around 60 to 70 per cent of operating expenditure, with the rest going to connectivity, cybersecurity, staffing and overheads.
"It makes sense to prioritise projects that bring stronger technological spillovers, better efficiency, and more meaningful contributions to the domestic ecosystem rather than simply approving every proposal," he told Business Times.
Malaysia has emerged as one of Southeast Asia's fastest-growing data centre markets in recent years, attracting investments from global hyperscalers and technology firms seeking alternatives to Singapore due to land and power constraints there.
In February 2026, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim had restricted approvals for non-AI data centre applications for almost two years as part of efforts to attract investments that deliver stronger technological spillovers and long-term economic value.
Hazim said the policy presents both opportunities and risks, noting that artificial intelligence (AI)-focused data centres serve a high-value market but are significantly more expensive to build and operate compared with conventional high-density facilities.
"If the country focuses too heavily on AI-specific capacity without sufficient underlying demand, that could create an imbalance.
"Malaysia should aim for a balanced approach," he added, supporting AI-related development while continuing to accommodate high-density data centres that serve a broader customer base and offer more flexibility to the market.
However, Hazim said local participation in the sector's supply chain, particularly among small and medium enterprises and Bumiputera technology firms, remains uneven despite rapid expansion.
"We believe there should be stronger mechanisms to ensure that local companies are not sidelined and are able to participate more meaningfully in this growth," he said.
Despite tighter policies and growing scrutiny over electricity and water consumption, Hazim said Malaysia remains well-positioned to attract long-term data centre investments, supported by its land availability, energy security, economic stability and geopolitical neutrality.
"As long as Malaysia remains consistent and credible in these areas, it should continue to be an attractive destination for data centre investment," he added.
Hazim said Malaysia's policy shift towards prioritising artificial intelligence (AI)-related data centres is creating new demands for local engineering firms, requiring them to keep pace with evolving technologies and international delivery standards.
In practice, this requires closer collaboration with technology providers, many of whom are based in the United States (US) or China, he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WCE Section 3 completion lifts IJM outlook, says CIMB
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The completion of Section 3 of the West Coast Expressway (WCE) is expected to remove a key overhang for IJM Corp Bhd, according to CIMB Securities Research.
The long-delayed stretch is moving closer to full operation.
Section 3 of the WCE is scheduled to open to traffic in the first quarter of 2027, marking a key milestone in the highway's development and improving west coast connectivity.
CIMB Securities said construction progress has accelerated following a breakthrough in land acquisition disputes with 19 residents in Kampung Jawa, Klang, in December 2025.
Long regarded as a missing link in the WCE route, Section 3 will complete the connection between the first two sections of the highway.
"This will improve traffic flow and connectivity for heavy vehicles travelling between northern and southern Peninsular Malaysia.
"Once operational, Section 3 is expected to solidify WCE's role as a viable alternative to the North-South Expressway, easing congestion and improving traffic distribution along the west coast corridor."
While the near-completion of the WCE removes a key overhang for the stock, CIMB Securities has cut its fiscal years 2026 to 2028 core profit forecasts for IJM by 4.6 per cent, 19.4 per cent and 19.9 per cent respectively.
Forecasts now stand at RM321 million, RM367 million and RM409 million.
The research firm said this reflects higher cost pressures stemming from the Middle East conflict and a two to five per cent decline in Kuantan Port cargo volumes.
Following the earnings adjustments, CIMB Securities cut its target price for IJM to RM2.90 from RM3.50.
It maintained a "buy" recommendation on the stock.
This is contingent on greater clarity on potential value-unlocking initiatives, which may include a spin-off of its construction and toll concession businesses, divestment of its Indian operations, and further capital management measures.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chemical sector plays quiet but critical role in Malaysia's economic growth
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's electronics and semiconductor industries often dominate discussions on the country's economic growth, but industry players say the chemical sector plays a less visible yet equally important role in generating domestic economic value.
The sector also supports high-skilled jobs and strengthens industrial resilience.
A recent analysis by the World Bank highlights this hidden strength using a concept called Domestic Value Added (DVA), a measure of how much money from a product actually stays in Malaysia versus how much leaks out to foreign countries.
While Malaysia's electrical and electronics (E&E) industry remains the country's largest export engine, it retains about 54 per cent of its economic value domestically, reflecting its reliance on imported components and machinery for assembly and re-export.
By comparison, the chemical sector retains about 61 per cent of its value within Malaysia, underscoring its deeper integration into the domestic economy.
According to Ancom Nylex Bhd chief executive officer Datuk Lee Cheun Wei, the stronger value retention reflects the structure of the industry, which functions as a critical midstream engine that transforms raw inputs into higher-value outputs.
"We take primary feedstocks, whether sourced locally or imported, plus intermediates which are mostly locally produced, and subject them to complex industrial processes and proprietary formulations to create specialised outputs," Lee said.
Because this process requires expensive, heavy-duty factories and high-level technical expertise, a much larger slice of the economic pie stays within the country.
He added that fields like fertiliser production and speciality chemical manufacturing require a highly skilled local workforce, including chemists, engineers, and technical teams.
By growing this industry, Malaysia isn't just making products; it is building a community of high-skilled professionals and keeping top-tier job opportunities local, he said.
The Economy's Unsung Hero
Lee described the chemical industry as the "invisible backbone" supporting almost every other major sector in Malaysia.
In agriculture, it supplies fertilisers and herbicides critical for palm oil and food production, while in manufacturing and construction it provides resins, adhesives and coatings. The semiconductor industry, meanwhile, depends on high-purity solvents and speciality gases.
"Without a strong local chemical sector, these critical pillars of the Malaysian economy would be forced to rely on expensive, complicated imports from overseas," Lee said.
Despite its strengths, the industry faces challenges in moving up the value chain, particularly in developing advanced materials that require heavy investment in research and development as well as specialised production facilities.
Global uncertainties are also adding pressure, with disruptions in major shipping lanes and geopolitical tensions in the Middle East driving volatility in energy prices and increasing logistics and insurance costs.
Lee said these unpredictable global events make it incredibly difficult for companies to plan long-term investments.
Despite the rough waters, local players see a bright future.
As Southeast Asia demands better food security and the global tech world demands more advanced microchips, the need for high-end chemicals is skyrocketing, said an industry insider.
"By carving out a specialised niche in ultra-pure chemicals, Malaysia's chemical sector is positioning itself to capture an even bigger, more lucrative slice of the global supply chains," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Price Is What You Pay, Value Is What You Get
Article:
Legendary investor Warren Buffett once said, "Price is what you pay; value is what you get." It is one of the simplest yet most powerful ideas in business and investing. The statement reminds us that the amount of money spent on something does not necessarily reflect its true worth. In a world obsessed with discounts, luxury branding, and short-term gains, understanding the difference between price and value has become more important than ever.
The price is easy to identify. It is printed on the label, displayed on the stock exchange, or stated in a contract. Value, however, is deeper and often invisible at first glance. Value includes quality, durability, usefulness, trust, future benefits, and emotional satisfaction. Two products may carry the same price but provide completely different levels of value.
Consider a simple example involving shoes. A person may buy a cheap pair of shoes for RM80 because the price appears attractive. However, after a few months, the shoes wear out and need replacing. Another person buys a higher-quality pair for RM300 that lasts for years and provides comfort and durability. Although the second pair costs more upfront, it delivers far greater value over time. The cheaper option was low in price but expensive in the long run.
The same principle applies strongly in investing. Many inexperienced investors are attracted to low-priced stocks because they appear "cheap". A stock trading at RM0.20 may look more affordable than one trading at RM50. However, price alone tells us nothing about the quality of the company. A weak company with poor management, declining profits, and mounting debt may destroy shareholder wealth even if its stock price is very low. On the other hand, an excellent company with strong competitive advantages and consistent earnings may create enormous long-term value despite appearing expensive initially.
For example, some Malaysian companies have often traded at valuations that seemed high at the time. Yet investors who focused on their long-term value — innovation, customer loyalty, strong cash flows, and dominant market positions — were rewarded handsomely over the years. Meanwhile, many "cheap" speculative stocks eventually collapsed because they lacked real business value.
This distinction is equally important in business decisions. Some companies focus excessively on cutting costs to offer the lowest prices. While low prices may attract customers temporarily, businesses that sacrifice quality, service, or reliability often struggle to retain loyalty. Customers eventually realise that value matters more than price.
Take the airline industry as an example. Budget airlines attract travellers with low ticket prices, but hidden fees, delays, and poor service can create frustration. In contrast, premium airlines may charge more but provide reliability, comfort, better customer support, and overall peace of mind. Many travellers are willing to pay higher prices because they perceive greater value.
Education provides another powerful illustration. A professional training programme may appear expensive at first glance. Yet if the programme improves a person's skills, increases confidence, and enhances career opportunities, the long-term value may far exceed the initial fee. Conversely, a cheap programme with weak content and inexperienced trainers may waste both money and time. In this case, the true cost is not the price paid but the opportunity lost.
The concept also applies to relationships and reputation. In business, companies sometimes chase short-term profits at the expense of ethics and trust. The immediate financial gain may look attractive, but the long-term damage to reputation can be enormous. Corporate scandals across the world show how businesses that ignored integrity eventually paid a far greater price through lawsuits, fines, and loss of public confidence. Trust and credibility are intangible forms of value that cannot easily be measured but are incredibly important.
Consumers today are becoming increasingly aware of value beyond price. Many people are willing to support businesses that provide ethical sourcing, sustainability, and excellent customer service even if prices are slightly higher. They understand that value includes how a product is made, how employees are treated, and whether the business contributes positively to society.
The challenge, however, is that value often requires patience and critical thinking. Human psychology naturally gravitates toward bargains and immediate gratification. A low price creates excitement, while real value may take years to reveal itself. Successful investors, business leaders, and consumers learn to look beyond appearances. They ask deeper questions: Is this sustainable? Will this last? Does this improve my life or business over time?
Ultimately, the difference between price and value reflects the difference between short-term thinking and long-term wisdom. Price can fluctuate daily, but value tends to endure. Markets may become emotional, consumers may chase trends, and businesses may engage in price wars, but genuine value remains the foundation of lasting success.
"Price is what you pay; value is what you get" is therefore more than just an investing principle. It is a philosophy for making better decisions in business and life. Those who understand this distinction are less likely to be deceived by appearances and more likely to build lasting wealth, meaningful relationships, and sustainable success.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: The keys to Petronas
Article:
National oil companies do not exist to service the present. They exist to convert a finite and non-renewable resource into a permanent store of national capability, deployed across generations rather than drawn down within electoral cycles.
Every decision a national oil company makes about capital allocation, asset disposition, and counterparty selection is, in the deepest sense, a statement about how a country relates to time.
The question now confronting Malaysia, as two significant transactions with Saudi Aramco have made visible, is whether we are managing Petronas as a vehicle for long-term wealth creation or as a short-term source of petrodollars. The answer to that question is not hidden in the transaction documents. It is written into the fiscal relationship between Putrajaya and the company it owns.
Petronas was designed, through the Petroleum Development Act of 1974, to be Malaysia's most consequential institutional answer to the problem of time. The act vested the nation's hydrocarbon resources in a single custodian, insulated from short-term political pressure, mandated to develop those resources for the benefit of the Malaysian public across generations.
The founding logic was not complicated: oil is finite, the revenue it generates is temporary, and the institutional vehicle through which that revenue is managed will determine whether Malaysia emerges from the hydrocarbon era with permanent capabilities or merely with the memory of them.
Fifty years on, that founding logic is under sustained structural pressure that no single transaction reveals but which two recent transactions with Aramco make impossible to ignore.
In December 2025, for US$3 billion, Petronas divested a 20 per cent stake in its Canadian upstream and LNG positions to MidOcean Energy, a vehicle managed by Washington-based EIG Global Energy Partners, with 49 per cent of its funding anchored by Aramco and constituted of a coalition including Japanese energy companies whose primary motivation is security of LNG supply for their own domestic markets.
The announced rationale was portfolio optimisation and capital flexibility. The structural condition underlying it was this: Petronas revenues had declined by approximately 24 per cent in the first half of 2025, while the annual dividend obligation to Putrajaya, historically around RM32 billion, remained fixed. A constrained seller, transacting with a patient coalition with longer horizons, does not negotiate from strength, regardless of how disciplined its process.
Five months later, announced this week just as 2 million Muslims assemble in Saudi Arabia for the Haj, Aramco exited its 50 per cent equity stake in the Pengerang Refining and Petrochemical joint venture, transferring the position to Petronas, which now holds the asset in full.
The announced rationale was mutual strategic realignment. The structural condition was that Aramco's production had been compressed by roughly a third through Hormuz disruption and the ongoing regional conflict, making it a less reliable feedstock supplier to a refinery whose crude specifications were built around Gulf supply. Aramco shed the capital obligation of equity partnership while preserving the commercial supply contract.
Petronas absorbed 100 per cent of the equity risk at a moment when both the asset's revenues and Aramco's ability to supply it were structurally constrained. Unlike in Canada, the price which Petronas is paying Aramco was not revealed.
Viewed separately, each transaction is defensible. Viewed together, across the same counterparty, within five months, they reveal a directional pattern. In Canada, Petronas sold under fiscal pressure; a patient coalition with longer horizons bought. I
n Malaysia, Petronas absorbed equity risk during supply disruption; Aramco shed capital obligations while retaining commercial revenue. Each party acted within its constraints. Each party optimised for its own position, though Petronas, it must be acknowledged, did not always exercise the institutional resistance that its founding statute permits and its long-horizon mandate arguably required.
The asymmetry is that one party's constraints are externally imposed and annually recurring, while the other's are commercially manageable and long-horizon in nature. That asymmetry does not produce bad actors. It produces predictable outcomes.
The question this pattern raises is not one that Petronas's board can answer on its own. It is one that only the government can answer, because the government holds the key condition that determines everything else. When the annual fiscal extraction from Petronas exceeds the company's ability to reinvest for the future, the company shifts from being a vehicle for wealth creation to a mechanism for wealth distribution.
The distinction is not semantic. A company that creates wealth builds capabilities, acquires positions, and expands the national asset base available to future generations. A company that distributes historical wealth converts existing assets into current consumption, reduces the sovereign balance sheet available to future negotiations, and progressively narrows the range of choices available to those who will govern when the hydrocarbons run thin.
Malaysia is approaching that threshold, if it has not already crossed it. Without a growth agenda, fiscal extraction from a national oil company is not resource management. It is resource liquidation, conducted slowly and with institutional dignity, but liquidation nonetheless.
The government's relationship with Petronas is, in this sense, a precise reflection of its relationship with the wider economy. An economy that grows creates the fiscal space to allow its national champions to reinvest, to negotiate from strength, and to build positions that compound over decades.
An economy that prioritises distribution over generating growth converts its institutions into instruments of redistribution, drawing down tomorrow's endowment to manage today's politics. The work of that growth does not begin with Petronas. It begins with the conditions that allow the private sector to invest at scale, generating jobs, domestically captured revenues, and the corporate tax base that should be the government's principal return for being a credible public partner.
It requires the patient widening of direct and indirect taxation; sustained investment in the hard infrastructure of competitiveness and the soft infrastructure of human capability; and the institutional conditions within which a high-income society becomes possible.
Petronas must not be permitted to become the convenient casualty of a state that has not done the harder work of building growth elsewhere.
Petronas belongs to Malaysia. The question is whether Malaysia is managing it as an inheritance to be cultivated or a legacy to be spent.
Institutions endure not through the transactions they complete but through the clarity with which those who govern them understand what they are for.
==========================
*Datuk Omar Mustapha was formerly an independent non-executive director of Petronas (2009-2018), Chair of the Board Remuneration Committee and Chair of the Petronas Leadership Centre
